snippets.title: Defaults, keyword args and multiple returns
snippets.code:
def estimate(prompt, price_per_token=0.000002):
    tokens = len(prompt.split())
    cost = tokens * price_per_token
    return tokens, cost            # returns a tuple

tokens, cost = estimate("hello gen ai world")   # unpack
print(tokens, round(cost, 6))
print(estimate("hi", price_per_token=0.00001))  # keyword argument
snippets.explanation: Defaults make arguments optional; returning a tuple lets the caller unpack several results.

snippets.title: Forwarding options with *args and **kwargs
snippets.explanation: **kwargs captures arbitrary keyword options so a wrapper can pass them through without listing each one.

snippets.title: Scope, nonlocal and closures
snippets.code:
def make_counter():
    count = 0
    def increment():
        nonlocal count        # rebind the enclosing variable
        count += 1
        return count
    return increment

next_id = make_counter()
print(next_id(), next_id(), next_id())   # 1 2 3 -- state lives in the closure
snippets.explanation: The inner function closes over `count`; nonlocal lets it update the enclosing binding instead of creating a new local.

snippets.title: Functions as values: callbacks and tools
snippets.explanation: Typed functions with docstrings are exactly what LLM 'tool/function calling' exposes; callbacks pass behavior into other functions.
